title: Volcanoes

sources.label: USGS: Understanding volcanic hazards can save lives
USGS: Comprehensive monitoring provides timely warnings of volcano reawakening

review_notes: This is a simplified Year 4 model of a volcano cross-section.
Teacher should review local hazard examples before discussing local risk.
Diagram is deterministic SVG, not AI-generated imagery.

slides.title: Volcanoes
slides.subtitle: How magma, vents, craters, lava, and ash connect in an eruption.

slides.title: Understand the parts of a volcano and what happens during an eruption

cards.title: Name
cards.text: Identify key volcano parts on a labelled diagram.

cards.title: Explain
cards.text: Describe how magma can move upward through a vent.

cards.title: Connect
cards.text: Link eruption materials to hazards such as lava and ash.

slides.callout: Learning intention: use a diagram to explain how a volcano eruption happens.

slides.title: A volcano is an opening where hot material can reach the surface
slides.lead: Volcanoes form where molten rock, gases, and fragments can move from inside Earth to the surface.

bullets.title: Lava
bullets.text: Magma that has reached the surface.

bullets.title: Ash
bullets.text: Tiny fragments of rock and glass from explosive eruptions.

slides.source_note: Source-grounded summary from USGS Volcano Hazards Program pages.

slides.title: Volcano cross-section
slides.source_note: Deterministic SVG based on common volcano structure labels.

steps.title: Pressure builds
steps.text: Magma and gases collect below the volcano.

steps.title: Magma rises
steps.text: Molten rock moves upward through the main vent.

steps.title: Material erupts
steps.text: Lava, ash, and gases can come out through the crater or side vents.

items.title: Crater
items.text: Opening near the top of a volcano.

items.title: Magma chamber
items.text: Area where magma can collect below the surface.

items.title: Lava flow
items.text: Molten rock moving across the surface.

items.title: Ash cloud
items.text: Fine fragments carried upward during some eruptions.

items.title: Hazard
items.text: Something that can cause harm and needs planning.

slides.title: Different eruption materials create different hazards

pairs.effect: Can bury or burn things in their path.
Can affect air, water, engines, roofs, and breathing.
Can be dangerous near the volcano.

slides.source_note: Hazard examples should be reviewed with local context if relevant.

slides.title: A classroom diagram is useful, but simplified
slides.lead: Real volcanoes have different shapes, sizes, eruption styles, and hazards. This diagram shows common parts, not every volcano.

cards.title: Useful
cards.text: The model helps us see hidden parts inside a volcano.

cards.title: Limited
cards.text: It does not show the full range of volcano types or eruption behavior.

slides.title: Read the diagram like a scientist

steps.label: Locate
steps.title: Find the parts
steps.text: Point to the crater, main vent, magma chamber, lava flow, and ash cloud.

steps.label: Trace
steps.title: Follow the path
steps.text: Trace how magma could move from below Earth to the surface.

steps.label: Explain
steps.title: Use because
steps.text: Explain one hazard using a cause-and-effect sentence.

questions.q: What is the difference between magma and lava?
questions.a: Magma is below the surface; lava is at the surface.

questions.q: Which part is the main pathway upward?
Why can ash be a hazard?
questions.a: It can affect air, water, machines, buildings, and breathing.

slides.title: Show your understanding
prompts: One volcano part I can identify is...
One question I still have about volcanoes is...
slides.challenge: Challenge: explain why diagrams help us understand things we cannot easily see.

slides.title: Sources used
slides.note: Image policy: deterministic SVG diagram; no AI-generated factual volcano image.
